sets.passage:
[A] Economists have long puzzled over why some societies generate a steady stream of inventions while others stagnate despite comparable resources. The conventional answer once centred on individual genius, yet contemporary scholarship locates the engine of progress in institutions, incentives, and the willingness of investors to fund uncertain ventures. Innovation, on this account, is less a flash of inspiration than a social process embedded in markets and laws.

[B] Central to that process is the protection conferred by patents. By granting a temporary monopoly, the state allows an inventor to recoup the costs of research before rivals copy the idea freely. Critics counter that overly broad protection can choke later creators, who find themselves blocked by a thicket of overlapping claims. The optimal balance, most agree, lies somewhere between generous reward and unfettered imitation.

[C] Funding presents an equally delicate dilemma. Truly novel projects carry risks that ordinary lenders refuse to bear, since the chance of total loss is high. Venture capitalists emerged precisely to shoulder such gambles, spreading their bets across many start-ups in the hope that a single triumph will offset numerous failures. Their appetite for risk, though often criticised, underwrites work that cautious banks would never touch.

[D] The geography of invention also matters. Clusters such as Silicon Valley concentrate talent, capital, and tacit knowledge in a way that no isolated laboratory can replicate. When engineers move freely between firms, ideas leak across boundaries and recombine in unexpected ways, accelerating the pace at which problems are solved. Density, in short, breeds creativity.

[E] Yet not every innovation enriches the society that produces it. Some advances merely shift profits from one company to another without expanding the overall pie, a phenomenon economists label rent-seeking. Distinguishing genuinely productive breakthroughs from clever manoeuvres designed only to capture existing wealth remains a stubborn analytical challenge.

[F] Failure, paradoxically, is indispensable. A culture that punishes every unsuccessful attempt discourages the experimentation on which discovery depends. Economies that treat bankruptcy as a learning opportunity rather than a permanent stigma tend to spawn more ambitious entrepreneurs, because the downside of trying is bounded while the upside remains open.

[G] Governments influence the whole apparatus through their spending priorities. Public money for basic research, which private firms underfund because its benefits are diffuse, has seeded technologies from the internet to modern medicine. Such investment yields returns that no single company could capture, justifying collective support for knowledge whose payoff is unpredictable.

[H] Finally, the rewards of innovation are distributed unevenly, raising questions of fairness. While inventors and shareholders may prosper, workers whose skills become obsolete bear the costs of disruption. Whether the gains can be shared more broadly without dulling the incentives that drive progress is perhaps the defining economic question of the age.
statements: Workers whose abilities are rendered outdated by new technologies suffer losses even as others grow wealthy.
A monopoly granted for a limited period lets creators recover their research expenses before competitors imitate them.
Modern thinking attributes technological progress to social structures rather than to gifted individuals acting alone.
State funding for fundamental inquiry has given rise to technologies that private companies would not have financed.
Investors who deliberately accept high failure rates make possible projects that conventional lenders avoid.
Tolerating commercial collapse as a source of lessons encourages bolder risk-taking among founders.
Some advances simply transfer earnings between firms without enlarging total wealth.
Concentrating skilled people in one location allows knowledge to spread informally and combine in novel forms.
Excessively wide legal protection may obstruct subsequent inventors entangled in competing claims.
Telling apart truly productive breakthroughs from schemes aimed at seizing existing wealth is analytically difficult.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段指出技能被淘汰的工人承担损失，而股东等获益。2. B段说临时垄断让发明者在被模仿前收回研究成本。3. A段说当代学术把进步归于制度而非孤立的天才。4. G段说公共基础研究资金催生了私企不会资助的技术。5. C段说风险投资者甘冒高失败率，支持普通贷款人回避的项目。6. F段说把破产当作学习机会会鼓励更大胆的创业。7. E段说有些进步只是在公司间转移利润而不扩大总量（寻租）。8. D段说集中人才让知识非正式扩散并重新组合。9. B段说过宽的保护会阻碍陷入重叠权利主张的后来者。10. E段说区分真正高效的突破与攫取财富的手段在分析上很难。

sets.passage:
[A] Cities are not neutral backdrops to human life; their physical form quietly steers how residents move, meet, and behave. Planners increasingly recognise that the arrangement of streets and squares can encourage or discourage particular habits long before any rule is written. The built environment, in effect, governs by suggestion rather than by decree, shaping conduct through countless small nudges that most inhabitants never consciously notice.

[B] Consider the simple matter of walking. Neighbourhoods laced with short blocks and frequent crossings invite people onto their feet, while sprawling districts dominated by wide roads quietly compel reliance on the car. Where destinations cluster within strolling distance, residents accumulate exercise without intending to, and chance encounters multiply.

[C] Public spaces perform a subtler function still. A well-placed bench or a shaded plaza gives strangers a reason to linger, and lingering breeds the casual conversation from which a sense of community grows. Designers who strip away such pauses, fearing loiterers, often hollow out the very sociability that makes a district feel alive.

[D] Density shapes tolerance as well. Living in close proximity to people unlike oneself, repeatedly and unavoidably, tends to soften prejudice over time. The constant low-level negotiation of shared pavements and queues teaches a practical civility that homogeneous suburbs, where contact is optional, rarely cultivate. Researchers who track attitudes across decades observe that residents of mixed quarters grow noticeably more relaxed about difference than their counterparts in segregated enclaves.

[E] Yet proximity carries costs. Crowding, noise, and the relentless stimulation of the street can fray the nerves, and prolonged exposure has been linked to heightened stress and even to disturbed sleep. The same intensity that sparks creativity may also exhaust the mind over time, which is why ready access to quiet green refuges is not a luxury but a genuine psychological necessity for anyone who must endure the city day after day.

[F] Green spaces themselves do remarkable work. Even a modest park lowers measured anxiety and nudges people toward gentler interactions, as though the presence of trees recalibrates the temper. Studies repeatedly find that views of vegetation hasten recovery in hospitals and calm restless behaviour in schools.

[G] The architecture of fear deserves mention too. Dark underpasses and blank walls signal danger and prompt avoidance, reshaping the routes people dare to take after nightfall. Conversely, lit, overlooked streets reassure, and the mere sense of being watched by neighbours discourages petty crime more reliably than any patrol.

[H] Transport networks complete the picture. Whether a city privileges the private vehicle or the shared carriage determines not only commuting times but the texture of daily encounter. Systems that mix passengers across class and origin foster a fleeting solidarity that segregated highways, sealing each traveller in metal, systematically erode.
statements: Mixing travellers of different backgrounds aboard shared transport creates a brief sense of common belonging.
The way streets and open areas are laid out can promote certain behaviours before any regulation exists.
Repeated unavoidable contact with dissimilar neighbours gradually reduces prejudice.
Even a small park measurably reduces anxiety and prompts milder social behaviour.
Compact neighbourhoods with many crossings draw people to walk and increase casual meetings.
Streets that feel observed by residents deter minor crime better than policing.
Removing places to sit out of fear of loiterers destroys the sociability that animates an area.
The intense stimulation of urban streets can wear people down and raise stress levels.
Designers now accept that the built surroundings guide conduct through subtle suggestion rather than command.
Sealing each traveller inside a private car steadily weakens collective solidarity.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说混合不同阶层乘客的交通系统培育短暂的团结感。2. A段说街道与广场的布局在规则成文前就能促进特定习惯。3. D段说与不同邻居反复不可避免的接触会逐渐软化偏见。4. F段说即便小公园也能降低焦虑并促使更温和的互动。5. B段说短街区、多路口的社区吸引人们步行并增加偶遇。6. G段说被邻居注视的街道比巡逻更能抑制轻微犯罪。7. C段说出于怕闲人而去掉长椅会瓦解使街区有活力的社交。8. E段说城市街道的强烈刺激会让人疲惫并提高压力。9. A段说设计者现在接受建成环境通过暗示而非命令引导行为。10. H段说把旅客封在私家车里会侵蚀集体团结。

sets.passage:
[A] The mass collection of personal data has become the quiet infrastructure of modern life, yet the ethical questions it raises are anything but settled. Every click, purchase, and movement now leaves a trace that some organisation may store, analyse, and sell to whoever will pay. The convenience this enables is real and considerable, but so too is the deepening unease about who ultimately controls the permanent record of our private lives.

[B] Consent lies at the heart of the dispute. Companies routinely obtain permission through dense agreements that almost no one reads, then treat that perfunctory click as genuine authorisation. Ethicists argue that consent extracted without real understanding is hollow, amounting to a legal fiction rather than a meaningful choice.

[C] A further worry concerns the gap between collection and use. Information gathered for one harmless purpose may later be repurposed in ways the subject never anticipated, such as when shopping habits are mined to infer health conditions. This drift of data beyond its original justification erodes any pretence that the original agreement still applies.

[D] Anonymisation was once offered as a reassuring safeguard, but its promise has proven fragile. Researchers have repeatedly shown that supposedly anonymous records can be cross-referenced with other datasets to re-identify individuals with disturbing ease. The mathematical reality is that stripping names rarely guarantees true invisibility, since a handful of seemingly innocuous attributes can together single out one person among millions with unsettling precision.

[E] Power asymmetry deepens the problem. The institutions amassing data possess analytical capabilities that ordinary citizens cannot match, leaving individuals unable to grasp, let alone contest, how they are profiled. This imbalance turns what is described as a voluntary exchange into something closer to quiet coercion.

[F] Defenders of the practice point to tangible benefits. Aggregated data can expose disease outbreaks, optimise traffic, and tailor education to individual needs, achievements that would be impossible without large-scale collection. To forbid gathering altogether, they insist, would sacrifice genuine social goods on the altar of an abstract principle.

[G] The question of ownership remains unresolved. If data describes a person, does that person retain a claim over it, or does the entity that records and structures it acquire the rights? Different legal traditions answer this differently, and the absence of consensus leaves individuals uncertain of what, if anything, they may demand.

[H] Looking ahead, many scholars urge a shift from seeking permission to enforcing accountability. Rather than burdening users with choices they cannot evaluate, this approach would hold organisations strictly responsible for harm, regardless of whatever consent they claim to have secured. Responsibility, not ritual agreement, becomes the firm moral anchor of the whole arrangement.
statements: Holding firms answerable for damage, instead of relying on users' permission, is proposed as a better ethical foundation.
Data obtained for an innocent reason can later be exploited in ways the person never foresaw.
Supporters note that pooled records can detect epidemics and improve public services.
The endless trail left by everyday actions can be stored and traded by some organisation.
Agreements buried in unreadable terms make the resulting consent essentially meaningless.
Records said to be nameless can often be linked to other data and re-identified.
It is unclear whether a person keeps a claim over data describing them or the recorder owns it.
Those collecting information have analytical powers individuals cannot rival, making the exchange coercive.
The benefits of constant collection are genuine, yet so is the anxiety over who controls our records.
Forbidding all data gathering would, defenders argue, waste real social benefits for an abstraction.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段主张让机构对损害负责，而非依赖用户许可，作为更好的伦理基础。2. C段说为无害目的收集的数据可能被以本人未预料的方式利用。3. F段说聚合数据能发现疫情并改进公共服务。4. A段说日常行为留下的痕迹会被某组织存储和出售。5. B段说埋在无人阅读条款中的同意实质上毫无意义。6. D段说号称匿名的记录常能与其他数据交叉重新识别。7. G段说尚不清楚个人是否对描述自己的数据保有权利还是记录者拥有。8. E段说收集者具备个人无法匹敌的分析能力，使交换近乎胁迫。9. A段说便利真实，但对谁控制我们记录的不安也真实。10. F段说支持者认为全面禁止收集会为抽象原则牺牲真实社会利益。

sets.passage:
[A] For most of the twentieth century, learning was imagined as the steady pouring of facts into a passive mind, much as water might be poured into an empty vessel waiting to be filled. Recent research in cognitive science has decisively overturned that picture, revealing the brain instead as an active, selective organ that constructs understanding rather than simply absorbing it. How we study, it now turns out, matters fully as much as what we study.

[B] One of the most robust findings concerns the value of difficulty. When recalling information requires effort, the resulting memory proves far more durable than when the same material is merely reread. Struggling to retrieve an answer, far from being a sign of failure, is precisely what strengthens the neural pathway and cements the knowledge.

[C] Spacing offers a related lesson. Cramming material into a single session produces a flattering but fleeting sense of mastery, whereas distributing the same study across several days yields retention that lasts. The brain appears to consolidate during the intervals, so the pauses between sessions are not wasted time but essential processing.

[D] Sleep performs work that no amount of waking effort can replace. During the night the brain replays and reorganises the day's experiences, transferring fragile new memories into stable long-term storage. Students who sacrifice rest to study longer often undermine the very consolidation they are straining to achieve.

[E] Mixing topics, counterintuitively, sharpens learning. When problems of different kinds are interleaved rather than practised in tidy blocks, learners are forced to identify which approach each situation demands. This messier practice feels harder and slower, yet it builds the flexible judgement that real-world tasks require.

[F] Emotion colours every act of remembering. Material that arouses curiosity or feeling lodges itself more firmly than dry, neutral content, because the chemicals released by engagement tag an experience as worth keeping. Teachers who awaken wonder are therefore not merely entertaining but exploiting a fundamental property of memory.

[G] Belief about ability shapes outcomes too. Learners who regard intelligence as something that grows with effort persist through setbacks, while those who view it as fixed retreat at the first failure. The expectation a student holds about their own capacity can quietly become a self-fulfilling prophecy.

[H] Finally, explaining an idea to someone else mercilessly exposes the gaps in one's own grasp. The very act of teaching forces a learner to organise scattered fragments into a single coherent account, and the awkward holes that surface during that effort mark out, with unusual precision, exactly where understanding is still incomplete and further study is required.
statements: Trying to teach an idea reveals where one's own understanding remains unfinished.
Memory formed through effortful recall lasts far longer than memory from simple rereading.
Science now portrays the brain as actively building understanding instead of passively receiving it.
Believing ability grows with effort helps learners endure setbacks and can fulfil itself.
Sleep transfers fragile new memories into durable storage in a way waking study cannot.
Practising several different problem types together forces learners to judge which method fits.
Spreading study across several days produces longer retention than concentrating it in one sitting.
Content that stirs curiosity or feeling is retained more firmly than neutral material.
Effortful retrieval, rather than signalling failure, is what reinforces the neural connection.
Distributing practice over time works because the brain consolidates during the gaps between sessions.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说向他人讲解会暴露自己理解的缺口。2. B段说通过费力回忆形成的记忆远比简单重读持久。3. A段说科学现在把大脑视为主动建构理解而非被动接收。4. G段说相信能力随努力增长有助于熬过挫折并可能自我实现。5. D段说睡眠以清醒学习无法替代的方式把脆弱新记忆转入稳固存储。6. E段说把不同题型混合练习迫使学习者判断该用哪种方法。7. C段说把学习分散到数天比集中一次保留更久。8. F段说激起好奇或情感的内容比中性材料记得更牢。9. B段说费力提取不是失败信号而是强化神经连接。10. C段说分散练习有效是因为大脑在间隔中巩固。

sets.passage:
[A] Biodiversity, the staggering variety of living forms that share the planet, is too often treated as a pleasant adornment rather than as a foundation of human survival itself. Yet the intricate web of species sustaining clean air, fertile soil, and stable harvests is far more than mere scenery to be admired. Understanding its true worth requires looking past the charismatic animals that fill documentaries toward the unseen machinery of life on which everything else quietly depends.

[B] Consider the services that ecosystems quietly provide. Wetlands filter pollutants from water, forests regulate rainfall, and insects pollinate the crops that feed billions, all without invoice or contract. Economists who have attempted to price these functions arrive at figures so vast that markets, accustomed to ignoring them, can scarcely comprehend the scale.

[C] Variety also confers resilience. A field sown with a single crop may collapse when one pest arrives, whereas a diverse system contains organisms that buffer shocks and fill gaps when others fail. This redundancy, far from being wasteful, is precisely the insurance that allows nature to withstand disturbance.

[D] Medicine owes an enormous and underappreciated debt to wild organisms. A large share of pharmaceuticals derives from compounds first discovered in plants, fungi, or marine creatures, and countless species remain chemically unexplored. Each extinction therefore forecloses cures that might never now be found, a loss measured in lives unsaved.

[E] The genetic library held within wild relatives of domestic crops is equally precious. When disease threatens cultivated varieties, breeders turn to these hardy ancestors for traits of resistance bred out of pampered modern strains. Preserving such reservoirs is, in effect, safeguarding the future adaptability of agriculture itself.

[F] Beyond utility lies a cultural and aesthetic dimension. Human imagination has been shaped for millennia by the creatures and landscapes among which our species evolved, and many people feel an intuitive grief at their disappearance that no economic ledger captures. This attachment, though hard to quantify, is no less real.

[G] The threats, meanwhile, are accelerating. Habitat destruction, a warming climate, and the spread of invasive species are driving losses at a rate that scientists compare to the great extinctions of the geological past. What distinguishes the present episode is that a single species, our own, is the principal cause.

[H] Conservation, encouragingly, can succeed handsomely when communities perceive a direct and tangible benefit. Where local people share generously in the rewards of protecting a forest or a coral reef, they reliably become its most effective and vigilant guardians, demonstrating that safeguarding nature need not conflict with human prosperity at all but can in fact quietly underpin and strengthen it.
statements: Conservation works best when nearby communities gain tangible rewards from protecting nature.
A field of a single crop may fail when one pest strikes, while varied systems absorb such shocks.
The diversity of life is wrongly viewed as decoration rather than a basis of human survival.
Wild relatives of crops supply disease-resistant traits lost in pampered modern varieties.
Wetlands, forests, and insects deliver functions whose economic value is almost beyond comprehension.
Many people feel an instinctive sorrow at species loss that no financial accounting can capture.
A large portion of medicines comes from compounds first found in wild plants, fungi, or sea creatures.
Current extinction rates rival ancient geological catastrophes, but one species is now the chief cause.
The redundancy within diverse systems acts as insurance against disturbance.
Each extinction may eliminate cures that will now never be discovered.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说当周边社区从保护自然中获得实际回报时保护最有效。2. C段说单一作物田遇一种害虫可能崩溃，而多样系统能吸收冲击。3. A段说生物多样性被误当作点缀而非人类生存的基础。4. E段说作物野生近亲提供现代品种已丧失的抗病性状。5. B段说湿地、森林和昆虫提供的功能其经济价值几乎无法估量。6. F段说许多人对物种消失有金钱核算无法捕捉的本能悲伤。7. D段说大部分药物源自野生植物、真菌或海洋生物中首次发现的化合物。8. G段说当前灭绝速率堪比远古地质灾难，但人类是主因。9. C段说多样系统的冗余充当抵御扰动的保险。10. D段说每次灭绝都可能消除如今再也无法发现的疗法。

sets.passage:
[A] The workplace is undergoing a transformation as profound and far-reaching as the historic shift from farm to factory two centuries ago. Automation, instant networked communication, and rapidly changing expectations are together dissolving the comfortable assumptions that organised labour for generations. What it means to hold a job, and indeed where that job actually happens, is being quietly but thoroughly rewritten before our eyes.

[B] The most visible change is the loosening of place. Once tethered to a single office, many employees now work from wherever a connection reaches, a freedom that the recent global disruptions accelerated dramatically. Companies that resisted remote arrangements discovered, often to their surprise, that productivity did not collapse when the desks emptied.

[C] Yet distance exacts a hidden price. The casual exchanges that once happened by the coffee machine, where problems were solved and trust was built, do not easily survive the move to scheduled video calls. Managers increasingly worry that the spontaneous collaboration on which innovation depends withers when colleagues never share a room.

[D] Automation reshapes the content of work as much as its location. Tasks that are routine and predictable are steadily handed to machines, leaving humans to concentrate on judgement, empathy, and creativity that software cannot yet replicate. The fear of wholesale replacement, however, often overlooks how technology tends to transform jobs rather than abolish them.

[E] The very structure of employment is fragmenting. The lifelong contract with a single employer is giving way to a patchwork of short engagements, freelance projects, and gig assignments. This flexibility liberates some workers while stripping others of the security, pensions, and protections that the old arrangement quietly guaranteed.

[F] Skills now expire with alarming speed. The expertise that secured a career at twenty-five may be obsolete by forty, forcing workers to learn continuously rather than once. Organisations that thrive are those treating training not as an occasional expense but as a permanent condition of remaining relevant.

[G] The boundary between work and life, meanwhile, grows perilously thin. When the office travels home in a pocket-sized device, the working day no longer ends at a fixed hour, and the resulting exhaustion has prompted some governments to legislate a right to disconnect. Constant availability, it turns out, carries a steep human cost.

[H] Finally, what workers actually want from employment has shifted in fundamental ways. A younger generation increasingly weighs purpose, autonomy, and personal well-being against salary alone, and employers who carelessly ignore these new priorities now struggle badly to attract and keep the best talent. The implicit contract between company and individual is being quietly renegotiated around meaning, not merely money.
statements: Younger workers increasingly value purpose and autonomy over pay, reshaping the employer relationship.
Freed from a fixed office, many now work anywhere with a connection, a shift recent disruptions hastened.
Expertise grows obsolete so fast that workers must keep learning rather than train only once.
The change reshaping work rivals the historic move from agriculture to industry.
Routine, predictable tasks pass to machines, leaving people the judgement software cannot copy.
The informal exchanges that built trust struggle to survive a switch to scheduled video calls.
Always being reachable carries such a cost that some governments legislate a right to disconnect.
Lifelong employment with one firm is dissolving into short freelance and gig engagements.
Firms that prosper treat training as a permanent necessity, not an occasional cost.
Many companies found productivity did not fall when offices emptied of staff.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说年轻员工更看重意义与自主而非薪水，重塑雇佣关系。2. B段说摆脱固定办公室后许多人在任何有网络处工作，近期动荡加速了这一转变。3. F段说专长很快过时，工人必须持续学习而非只培训一次。4. A段说这场变革堪比从农业到工业的历史转变。5. D段说常规可预测任务交给机器，留给人软件无法复制的判断。6. C段说建立信任的非正式交流难以在定时视频会议中存活。7. G段说始终可被联系代价巨大，以致一些政府立法规定离线权。8. E段说与单一公司的终身雇佣瓦解为短期自由职业和零工。9. F段说兴旺的公司把培训当作长期必需而非偶尔开支。10. B段说许多公司发现办公室空置时生产力并未下降。

sets.passage:
[A] Persuasion has fascinated thinkers since antiquity, but only in recent decades has psychology carefully mapped the hidden levers that move people toward agreement. Far from being purely a matter of rational argument, the delicate act of changing minds turns out to depend on a whole host of automatic tendencies that operate quietly beneath conscious awareness. To understand influence properly is to glimpse just how easily human reason can be bypassed altogether.

[B] One of the most powerful of these tendencies is reciprocity. When someone does us a favour, however small, we feel an almost physical obligation to return it, a reflex that skilled persuaders exploit by offering a token gift before making a request. The free sample is not generosity but strategy, priming the recipient to comply.

[C] Social proof operates with similar stealth. Uncertain how to act, people instinctively look to the behaviour of others, assuming that a crowd cannot be wrong. This is why advertisers trumpet popularity and queues attract more queuers; the mere appearance of consensus manufactures the very agreement it claims to report.

[D] Scarcity warps judgement in a related way. The moment an opportunity is framed as limited or fleeting, its perceived value swells, regardless of its actual merit. Marketers manufacture this urgency with countdowns and dwindling stock, knowing that the fear of missing out overrides sober calculation.

[E] Authority commands a deference that is largely unexamined. A message delivered by someone in a uniform, a title, or a lab coat is accepted more readily than the identical claim from an anonymous source. The symbols of expertise, even when borrowed or faked, can switch off the critical scrutiny a listener would otherwise apply.

[F] Consistency, too, binds people more tightly than they realise. Having taken a small public stance, individuals feel compelled to act in ways that confirm it, lest they appear unreliable. Cunning persuaders therefore secure a trivial initial commitment, confident that larger concessions will follow to preserve the appearance of integrity.

[G] Liking smooths the path to agreement in obvious yet underestimated ways. We say yes more readily to those we find attractive, familiar, or similar to ourselves, which is why salespeople mirror our gestures and emphasise shared interests. Rapport, manufactured or genuine, lowers resistance before any argument is made.

[H] Awareness of these mechanisms offers a measure of protection. Recognising that a feeling of obligation or urgency may have been deliberately engineered allows a person to pause and ask whether the underlying offer truly merits assent. Defence against manipulation begins, in short, with naming it. Those who study these triggers in advance report that simply labelling a tactic as it unfolds drains much of its force, restoring the deliberate reflection that automatic compliance would otherwise suppress.
statements: Recognising that a sense of urgency may be engineered lets a person pause and reassess the offer.
Mapping the hidden levers of influence reveals how readily reason can be circumvented.
A small favour creates an almost physical urge to repay it, which persuaders exploit with token gifts.
Once people take a small public stance, they feel bound to act consistently with it.
Uncertain people copy the crowd, assuming that many cannot be mistaken.
We agree more easily with those we find attractive or similar, so sellers stress shared interests.
Framing an opportunity as limited inflates its perceived value regardless of real merit.
Messages from someone bearing symbols of expertise bypass the scrutiny otherwise applied.
The appearance of consensus manufactures the very agreement it pretends merely to report.
Securing a trivial first commitment makes larger concessions likely as people protect their image.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说意识到紧迫感可能是被刻意制造的，让人停下重新评估。2. A段说揭示影响的隐藏杠杆显示理性多容易被绕过。3. B段说小恩惠制造几乎生理性的回报冲动，被说服者用小礼物利用。4. F段说人一旦公开表态就觉得必须保持一致。5. C段说不确定的人模仿人群，认为多数不会错。6. G段说我们更易认同有吸引力或相似者，故卖家强调共同点。7. D段说把机会框为有限会抬高其感知价值，无论真实价值。8. E段说带专业符号者的信息绕过本应有的审视。9. C段说共识的表象制造出它声称只是报告的认同。10. F段说先取得微小承诺会让更大让步随之而来以维护形象。

sets.passage:
[A] The way humanity powers its sprawling civilisation is now approaching a turning point every bit as decisive as the discovery of fire or the harnessing of steam. For roughly two centuries, fossil fuels reliably delivered cheap and abundant energy that built the modern world, but their hidden environmental costs have grown utterly impossible to ignore. The coming few decades will determine whether a genuinely cleaner system can be built fast enough to matter.

[B] Solar and wind have undergone a transformation few predicted. Once dismissed as expensive curiosities, they have plunged in price to become, in many regions, the cheapest electricity ever generated. The relentless decline in cost, driven by manufacturing scale and steady improvement, has quietly upended assumptions that dominated planning for decades.

[C] Their great weakness, however, is intermittency. The sun sets and the wind stills, yet demand persists, so a grid leaning heavily on these sources must somehow store energy or summon it from elsewhere. Solving this mismatch between supply and need is the central engineering puzzle of the transition.

[D] Batteries offer one answer, and their costs too have tumbled. Yet storing enough power to carry a city through a windless week, let alone a dark winter, demands quantities of storage that remain prohibitively expensive. The search continues for chemistries and methods cheap enough to bridge the longest gaps.

[E] Nuclear power presents a polarising alternative. It generates vast, steady, carbon-free electricity, immune to the whims of weather, yet public anxiety over accidents and waste has stalled its expansion in many countries. Whether a new generation of smaller, safer reactors can overcome this resistance remains genuinely uncertain.

[F] Hydrogen attracts growing interest as a flexible carrier of energy. Produced cleanly, it could fuel the heavy industries and long-haul transport that resist electrification, storing surplus power for later use. The obstacle is efficiency, since converting electricity to hydrogen and back wastes much of the original energy.

[G] The grid itself must be reinvented. A network designed for a few large, controllable power stations strains to accommodate millions of small, fluctuating sources scattered across the landscape. Smart systems that balance this chaos in real time are as essential to the transition as any single technology.

[H] Ultimately, the decisive barriers are less technical than they are political and social in nature. The practical tools needed to decarbonise the economy largely exist already, but deploying them at the necessary breakneck speed demands enormous investment, painstaking coordination, and a genuine willingness to overturn deeply entrenched interests. Whether societies can summon that collective resolve is the question on which the climate, in the end, depends.
statements: The decisive obstacles are political and social rather than technical, requiring resolve to overturn entrenched interests.
Solar and wind have fallen so far in price that they are now the cheapest electricity in many places.
The energy transition matters as much to civilisation as the mastery of fire or steam once did.
Hydrogen could fuel industries resistant to electrification, but conversion wastes much of the energy.
The variability of sun and wind forces a heavily reliant grid to store or import power.
Nuclear delivers steady carbon-free power but faces public fear over accidents and waste.
A network built for a few large stations struggles with millions of small fluctuating sources.
Batteries have grown cheaper, yet storing enough for a windless week remains prohibitively costly.
Falling costs driven by scale have quietly overturned long-held planning assumptions.
Smart real-time balancing systems are as vital to the transition as any single technology.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说决定性障碍是政治社会而非技术，需要决心推翻既得利益。2. B段说太阳能与风能价格大降，在许多地方成为最便宜的电力。3. A段说能源转型对文明的意义堪比掌握火或蒸汽。4. F段说氢能可为难以电气化的产业供能，但转换浪费大量能量。5. C段说太阳与风的间歇性迫使高度依赖的电网储存或进口电力。6. E段说核能提供稳定无碳电力但面临公众对事故与废料的恐惧。7. G段说为少数大电站设计的电网难以容纳数百万小型波动源。8. D段说电池虽更便宜，但储存足够撑过无风周仍代价高昂。9. B段说规模驱动的成本下降悄然推翻长期规划假设。10. G段说实时智能平衡系统对转型与任何单一技术同等重要。

sets.passage:
[A] Art is frequently dismissed as a luxury, an ornament affordable only after the serious business of survival is settled. Yet across every culture and epoch, human beings have devoted scarce resources to making and contemplating beauty, suggesting that the impulse answers a need far deeper than decoration. To ask what art is for is to ask what kind of creatures we are.

[B] One of its oldest functions is to bind communities together. Shared songs, festivals, and images forge a common identity, allowing strangers to recognise one another as members of a single people. Long before written law, it was ritual and story that transmitted the values holding a society in place.

[C] Art also serves as a vehicle for memory. Monuments, paintings, and epics preserve experiences that would otherwise vanish with the generation that lived them, carrying the triumphs and traumas of the past into the present. A culture without such records forgets itself, severed from the continuity that gives life meaning.

[D] Perhaps its subtlest power is the cultivation of empathy. By inviting an audience to inhabit a perspective not their own, a novel or a film stretches the imagination across the boundaries of class, era, and circumstance. Readers emerge having briefly lived another life, and that rehearsal of understanding spills into how they treat real people.

[E] Art frequently functions as dissent. Where direct criticism is dangerous, the oblique language of satire, song, and image can voice what cannot be said plainly, smuggling subversive truths past the censors. Tyrants have long feared the artist precisely because metaphor evades the controls that silence open speech.

[F] On a more personal level, the making of art offers a means of processing emotion. Giving shape to grief, fear, or joy through colour or melody allows feelings too large for words to be confronted and contained. The therapeutic power of this externalisation is now recognised in clinical settings as well as studios.

[G] Art additionally drives economic activity in ways often underestimated. Galleries, performances, and creative industries generate employment and draw visitors, while the design sensibility nurtured by artistic training quietly improves countless ordinary products. The cultural sector, far from being a drain, frequently repays public support many times over.

[H] Finally, art enlarges the very boundaries of perception. A painter who teaches us to notice a quality of light, or a composer who reveals a pattern in sound, permanently alters how we encounter the world thereafter. In this sense the artist is less an entertainer than an explorer, expanding the territory of what can be seen and felt.
statements: By revealing new qualities in light or sound, artists permanently change how we perceive the world.
Although treated as a luxury, art has consumed scarce resources in every culture, hinting at a deeper need.
By inviting audiences into other perspectives, stories stretch empathy across boundaries of class and era.
Galleries and creative industries generate jobs and often repay public support many times over.
Monuments and epics preserve experiences that would otherwise vanish with their generation.
Shared festivals and images forge a common identity that lets strangers recognise their shared belonging.
Where open criticism is perilous, oblique satire and image smuggle subversive truths past censors.
Giving shape to grief through colour or melody helps confront feelings too large for words.
Tyrants fear artists because metaphor evades the controls that silence plain speech.
Rehearsing understanding through fiction spills into how readers treat real people.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说艺术家通过揭示光或声的新特质永久改变我们对世界的感知。2. A段说艺术虽被当作奢侈，却在每种文化中耗费稀缺资源，暗示更深需求。3. D段说故事邀请观众进入他人视角，跨越阶级与时代延展共情。4. G段说画廊与创意产业创造就业，常数倍回报公共支持。5. C段说纪念碑与史诗保存本会随那代人消失的经历。6. B段说共同节庆与图像锻造共同身份，让陌生人相认。7. E段说在公开批评危险处，隐晦讽刺与图像把颠覆性真相偷渡过审查。8. F段说用色彩或旋律塑造悲伤有助直面言语难表的情感。9. E段说暴君惧怕艺术家因为隐喻能避开压制直言的控制。10. D段说通过虚构演练理解会渗入读者对真人的对待方式。

sets.passage:
[A] Sustainable development promises an appealing future in which prosperity, fairness, and a healthy planet all advance together, yet the comforting phrase conceals tensions that remain far from resolved. Reconciling the entirely legitimate desire of poorer nations to grow richer with the urgent global need to protect a finite environment has proven one of the most intractable challenges of the entire era. Good intentions, it turns out, are nowhere near enough.

[B] At the heart of the difficulty lies an apparent contradiction. For most of history, rising living standards have gone hand in hand with rising consumption of energy and materials, so demanding that developing countries lift their populations from poverty seems to clash directly with demands that they restrain their environmental footprint. Squaring this circle is the defining puzzle.

[C] One proposed escape is the notion of decoupling, the idea that economic growth can be severed from resource use through efficiency and clean technology. Optimists point to economies that have expanded while reducing certain emissions, yet sceptics note that much of this apparent progress merely exports the pollution to factories overseas.

[D] The question of fairness haunts every negotiation. The nations that industrialised first grew wealthy by burning fuels whose consequences the whole world now bears, leaving latecomers to wonder why they should forgo the same path. Any durable agreement must somehow acknowledge this uneven history of responsibility.

[E] Consumption patterns in rich societies compound the problem. A culture organised around constant acquisition and rapid disposal generates waste on a scale the planet cannot absorb, and no amount of efficiency offsets an appetite that expands without limit. Confronting overconsumption, however, collides with economies built on perpetual expansion.

[F] Cities occupy a pivotal position in any solution. As the share of humanity living in urban areas climbs, the design of those settlements will largely determine future demands on energy and land. Compact, well-served cities can shrink each resident's footprint, whereas sprawling ones lock in decades of waste.

[G] Measurement itself poses a quiet obstacle. The familiar yardstick of economic output ignores the depletion of forests, fisheries, and soils, flattering nations that are quietly liquidating their natural wealth. Until accounting captures these hidden losses, policy will continue to reward the very behaviour it ought to discourage.

[H] Ultimately, success may hinge on redefining what counts as a good life. If well-being can be uncoupled from ever-greater material throughput and located instead in health, relationships, and meaning, the conflict between prosperity and the planet softens. The deepest transformation required is therefore one of values, not merely technology.
statements: Lasting success may depend on relocating well-being in health and meaning rather than ever-more consumption.
Reconciling poorer nations' wish to grow with protecting a finite planet has proven deeply intractable.
Standard economic measures ignore the depletion of forests and soils, flattering nations liquidating natural wealth.
Compact, well-served cities can shrink each resident's footprint while sprawling ones lock in waste.
Decoupling growth from resource use is doubted because progress often just exports pollution abroad.
Nations that industrialised first prospered by burning fuels whose costs the whole world now bears.
Historically, rising living standards have accompanied rising consumption, clashing with calls for restraint.
A culture of constant acquisition generates waste that efficiency alone cannot offset.
Confronting overconsumption collides with economies built on endless expansion.
The urban share of humanity is rising, so settlement design will shape future demands on energy and land.
sets.explain_zh: 1. H段说持久成功或取决于把幸福重新定位于健康与意义而非更多消费。2. A段说调和较贫国家的增长愿望与保护有限星球极难解决。3. G段说标准经济指标忽视森林土壤的耗竭，美化在变卖自然财富的国家。4. F段说紧凑、服务完善的城市能缩小人均足迹，而蔓延城市锁定浪费。5. C段说脱钩受质疑因为进步常只是把污染输出到海外。6. D段说先工业化的国家靠燃烧燃料致富，其代价由全世界承担。7. B段说历史上生活水平上升伴随消费上升，与节制呼吁冲突。8. E段说不断获取的文化产生效率无法抵消的废物。9. E段说直面过度消费与建立在无限扩张上的经济相冲突。10. F段说城市化比例上升，故聚居设计将决定未来能源与土地需求。
